title: Minor Cuts and Scrapes
immediate_steps: Wash your hands before treating
Rinse the wound with clean water to remove dirt
Wash around the wound with mild soap
Apply gentle pressure with a clean cloth if bleeding hasn't stopped
Apply a thin layer of antibiotic ointment
Cover with a sterile bandage
avoid: Do not use alcohol, hydrogen peroxide, or iodine on the wound (they delay healing)
Do not blow on the wound

title: Severe External Bleeding
symptoms: Dizziness, confusion, or pale skin (signs of shock)
immediate_steps: Call emergency services immediately
Have the person lie down
Apply strong, direct pressure directly to the wound using a clean cloth or sterile dressing
If blood soaks through, place another cloth on top and press harder
Maintain constant pressure until help arrives
If the wound is on an arm or leg, elevate it above the heart if possible without causing pain
avoid: Do not remove the first layer of blood-soaked cloth
Do not check the wound to see if it has stopped bleeding
Do not use a makeshift tourniquet for non-life-threatening bleeding

title: Life-Threatening Bleeding (Tourniquet Required)
symptoms: Massive spurting blood from an arm or leg
Direct pressure fails to stop the bleeding
immediate_steps: Call emergency services immediately
Apply a commercially made tourniquet 2-3 inches above the bleeding site (not over a joint)
Tighten the tourniquet until the bleeding completely stops
Secure the tourniquet and note the exact time it was applied
If a commercial tourniquet is unavailable, use a wide strip of cloth and a stick to twist it tight (windlass)
Treat the person for shock
avoid: Do not apply a tourniquet over a joint (elbow or knee) - go above it
Do not loosen or remove the tourniquet once it is applied
Do not use narrow materials like wire or shoelaces (they cut tissue)

title: Nosebleed (Epistaxis)
symptoms: Blood flowing from one or both nostrils
immediate_steps: Have the person sit upright and lean slightly forward
Pinch the soft part of the nose just below the bony bridge
Breathe through the mouth
Hold pressure for at least 10-15 uninterrupted minutes
Apply a cold compress to the bridge of the nose
Seek medical care if bleeding lasts more than 20 minutes or follows a head injury
avoid: Do not lean the head back (blood will drain into the throat)
Do not blow the nose for several hours after bleeding stops

title: Bleeding - Types and Immediate Control
immediate_steps: Apply direct pressure on the wound using a clean cloth or bandage
Elevate the injured area above heart level if possible to reduce blood flow
Maintain steady pressure until bleeding slows or stops
Seek medical attention for severe or uncontrolled bleeding

title: Bleeding - Pressure and Dressing Techniques
immediate_steps: Apply firm and continuous direct pressure on the wound using a clean cloth, gauze, or bandage
If blood soaks through, add more layers on top instead of removing the original dressing
Maintain pressure until bleeding reduces significantly
If possible, elevate the injured limb above heart level to slow blood flow
Secure the dressing once bleeding is controlled
avoid: Do not frequently remove the cloth to check the wound, as this can restart bleeding
Avoid wrapping too tightly once secured

title: Bleeding - Severe Bleeding and Shock Prevention
immediate_steps: Apply strong direct pressure immediately
Keep the person lying down
Elevate the legs slightly if there are no suspected fractures
Keep the person warm using a blanket or clothing to maintain body temperature
Continue monitoring until emergency medical help arrives
avoid: Do not give food or water

title: Bleeding - Special Cases and Wound Care
immediate_steps: For embedded objects: stabilize it with padding around the object and apply pressure around the wound
For nosebleeds: pinch the soft part of the nose and lean forward slightly
For minor cuts: clean the wound with clean water and apply a sterile dressing
Always monitor for signs of infection such as redness, swelling, or pus after bleeding is controlled
avoid: Do not remove embedded objects as they may be controlling internal bleeding
For nosebleeds, do not lean back (prevents swallowing blood)
